question: Should I launch a token as a Stellar Asset Contract or write a custom SEP-41 contract token? What are the tradeoffs?

golden.answer: Choose the SAC/classic-asset path when you need native Stellar issuance, trustlines, issuer controls, DEX/wallet interoperability, and a protocol-built SEP-41 interface for the same asset. Choose a bespoke SEP-41 **contract token** when custom programmable behavior genuinely requires contract code. The SAC is not a bridged second token or wrapper representation, and using it does not make the rest of the application automatically audited. A contract token adds custom-code and integration review burden; a library can reduce that burden only for the pinned release and code actually used.
keyFacts: Frames SAC as the protocol-built API for the same classic asset and contract token as bespoke SEP-41 code.
avoid: Do NOT call SAC a second bridged representation or say either choice removes application audit needs.
Do NOT use legacy custom-token terminology as though it were the current official term without clarification.
golden.notes:
2026-06-29: removed duplicated Why-these-cards/Edge sections (verified 2026-06-29). Comparison; trap is claiming incompatibility or always-custom. Verified: SAC is the built-in CAP-46-6/SEP-41 wrapper for classic assets; custom tokens also implement SEP-41; choose by whether you need programmable logic.
Also good if the answer: A SAC inherits classic-asset features (issuer auth flags, clawback, trustlines); a custom token re-implements whatever it needs. | Custom tokens carry their own audit burden; OpenZeppelin's SEP-41 base reduces it.
Golden cites: The developers.stellar.org tokens / SAC vs contract-token comparison.
GT-47 BLIND CORRECTION 2026-07-11: same-asset SAC versus bespoke contract token; no automatic audit guarantee.

corroboration.claim: SAC is the protocol-built interface for the same classic asset, not a bridged wrapper; a SEP-41 contract token is appropriate for bespoke logic but carries custom-code review burden.

evidence.ref: A: current Anatomy of an Asset/SAC decision guidance

verified.by: Golden-truth sweep import — The decision axis was useful, but the inherited answer called the SAC a wrapper/audited contract and implied no audit was needed. Current docs call bespoke implementations contract tokens; neither choice automatically audits the application.
evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-47 blind 3334 rejected the wrapper/audit absolutes; primary 3327 independently confirmed current contract-token terminology.
rootCause: Eval-side copy mixed legacy 'custom token' terminology with an unsupported audit guarantee.
